description: Security issues in AWS Cognito User Pools including attribute manipulation, authentication bypass, and configuration weaknesses.

entries.name: Cognito Custom Attribute Self-Modification (Privilege Escalation)
entries.description: User can modify their own custom attributes (role, tenant_id) via the Cognito UpdateUserAttributes API because attributes are marked as mutable.
entries.how_it_works: Cognito custom attributes with mutable=True can be changed by the user using their access token. If the application trusts JWT claims from these attributes for authorization, the user can escalate privileges by changing custom:role to 'admin'.
preconditions: Application trusts JWT claim for authorization
User has a valid access token
entries.detection_guidance: In CDK: check if custom attributes use mutable=True. In Cognito console: check attribute settings. In authorizer code: check if role/tenant_id from JWT is trusted without policy store verification.
exploit_payloads:
# Python SDK
import boto3
client = boto3.client('cognito-idp')
client.update_user_attributes(
    AccessToken='user_access_token',
    UserAttributes=[{'Name': 'custom:role', 'Value': 'admin'}]
)
entries.safe_code:
# CDK: immutable custom attributes
user_pool = cognito.UserPool(self, 'Pool',
    custom_attributes={
        'tenant_id': cognito.StringAttribute(mutable=False),
        'role': cognito.StringAttribute(mutable=False),
    }
)

# OR: Use pre-token-generation trigger to inject authoritative claims
# OR: Verify claim against DynamoDB policy store
db_policy = policies_table.get_item(Key={'pk': f'USER#{user_id}'})['Item']
assert token_claims['custom:role'] == db_policy['role']
remediation: Set security-critical attributes to mutable=False
Use Cognito Groups for role management (groups can't be self-modified)
Add pre-token-generation Lambda trigger to inject authoritative claims
Verify JWT claims against a policy store on every request
Add write_attributes configuration to app client to restrict which attributes clients can write

entries.name: Cognito User Enumeration via SignUp/ForgotPassword
entries.description: Cognito's default error messages reveal whether an email/username exists, enabling user enumeration.
entries.how_it_works: SignUp returns 'UsernameExistsException' if user exists. ForgotPassword returns different messages for existing vs non-existing users. Attacker can enumerate valid emails.
preconditions: Cognito user pool without alias configuration hiding this
No CAPTCHA or rate limiting on signup/forgot-password
entries.detection_guidance: Check auth handler: does it return different error messages for 'user exists' vs 'user not found'? Does it expose Cognito's native error codes?
remediation: Return generic error messages (don't distinguish exists/not-exists)
Enable Cognito's PreventUserExistenceErrors setting
Add CAPTCHA after N attempts
Rate limit per IP on signup/forgot-password

entries.name: Cognito Without MFA Enforcement for Privileged Roles
entries.description: MFA is optional, allowing admin and compliance_manager accounts to operate without second factor.
entries.how_it_works: With MFA optional, users can skip MFA setup. If an admin's password is compromised (phishing, credential stuffing), there's no second factor to prevent account takeover.
preconditions: Privileged roles (admin, compliance_manager) exist without MFA mandate
impact: Admin account takeover via single factor
entries.detection_guidance: Check Cognito configuration: mfa=cognito.Mfa.OPTIONAL or mfa not specified. For compliance platforms, admin roles MUST have MFA.
exploit_payloads:
# Credential stuffing against admin account
# With optional MFA, only password needed
entries.safe_code:
user_pool = cognito.UserPool(self, 'Pool',
    mfa=cognito.Mfa.REQUIRED,
    mfa_second_factor=cognito.MfaSecondFactor(
        sms=False,  # SMS is insecure (SIM swap)
        otp=True,   # TOTP apps (Authenticator)
    ),
)
remediation: Set MFA to REQUIRED for all users, or at minimum for admin/compliance_manager roles
Use TOTP (not SMS) for MFA
Enable adaptive MFA (risk-based) if full MFA is too disruptive
For compliance platforms: MFA is a SOC2 requirement for privileged access

entries.name: Cognito App Client Without Read/Write Attribute Restrictions
entries.description: App client has access to read/write all user attributes, including security-critical ones.
entries.how_it_works: Cognito app clients can be configured with read_attributes and write_attributes to restrict which attributes the client can access. Without this, the frontend can read all attributes and potentially write to sensitive ones.
preconditions: App client without explicit attribute restrictions
Frontend has access to access token
impact: Potential attribute manipulation via client SDK
entries.detection_guidance: Check app client configuration: are read_attributes and write_attributes explicitly set? Or does it default to all?
entries.vulnerable_code:
app_client = user_pool.add_client('AppClient',
    auth_flows=[cognito.AuthFlow.USER_PASSWORD_AUTH],
    # No read_attributes or write_attributes specified — defaults to ALL
)
remediation: Explicitly set read_attributes and write_attributes on app client
Exclude security-critical attributes from write_attributes
Use separate app clients for admin operations vs user operations

entries.name: Cognito Custom Auth Flow Without Challenge Validation
entries.description: Custom authentication flow (CUSTOM_AUTH) with improperly implemented Define/Create/Verify challenge triggers allows authentication bypass.
entries.how_it_works: Custom auth flows use Lambda triggers to define challenges. If the VerifyAuthChallengeResponse trigger doesn't properly validate the answer, or if DefineAuthChallenge skips challenges under certain conditions, authentication can be bypassed.
entries.detection_guidance: Check if CUSTOM_AUTH is enabled. If so, review all Lambda triggers for logic flaws. Common issue: VerifyAuthChallengeResponse returns answerCorrect=true without proper validation.
entries.safe_code:
# VerifyAuthChallengeResponse trigger
def handler(event, context):
    expected = event['request']['privateChallengeParameters']['answer']
    actual = event['request']['challengeAnswer']
    event['response']['answerCorrect'] = (expected == actual)
    return event
remediation: Thoroughly review all custom auth Lambda triggers
Ensure VerifyAuthChallengeResponse ALWAYS validates the answer
Prefer standard auth flows (USER_PASSWORD_AUTH, USER_SRP_AUTH) unless custom is truly needed
Add integration tests for auth challenge edge cases

entries.name: Cognito Group Misconfiguration — Self-Join to Admin Group
entries.description: If the application allows users to specify their group at signup or the app client has admin API access, users can add themselves to privileged groups.
entries.how_it_works: Cognito Groups are used for authorization. If the AdminAddUserToGroup API is accessible from user-facing code, or if signup logic doesn't validate group assignment, users can join admin groups.
preconditions: OR: group assignment logic doesn't validate against invitation/approval
entries.detection_guidance: Check: does the auth Lambda have AdminAddUserToGroup permission? If so, is it gated by proper authorization (only admins can add to groups)?
entries.vulnerable_code:
# Auth handler: any authenticated user can assign groups
def assign_group(body):
    cognito.admin_add_user_to_group(
        UserPoolId=POOL_ID,
        Username=body['username'],
        GroupName=body['group']  # User controls which group!
    )
remediation: Restrict AdminAddUserToGroup to admin-only Lambdas
Validate group assignment against allowed groups for the requester's role
Use invitation flows for privileged group membership
Audit group membership changes
